questions.text: A pump delivers 350 gpm. What is this flow expressed in MGD?
A plant treats 2.0 MGD. What is the average flow in gpm?
Your crew drains 500 cubic feet of water out of a storage basin for an inspection. How much water went out, in gallons?
questions.explanation: 500 cu ft x 7.48 gal/cu ft = 3,740 gallons.

questions.text: A pressure gauge reads 45 psi. What is the equivalent head in feet of water?
questions.explanation: 45 psi x 2.31 ft/psi = 103.95, about 104 feet of head.

questions.text: A circular clarifier is 60 ft in diameter. What is its surface area?
A rectangular sedimentation basin is 80 ft long and 30 ft wide. What is its surface area?
questions.explanation: Area = length x width = 80 x 30 = 2,400 sq ft.

questions.text: A rectangular tank is 50 ft long, 20 ft wide, and 12 ft deep. What is its volume in gallons?
questions.formula: gallons = L x W x D x 7.48

questions.text: A cylindrical tank is 40 ft in diameter and 20 ft deep. What is its volume in gallons?
questions.formula: gallons = 0.785 x diameter squared x depth x 7.48

questions.text: How many gallons are contained in 1,000 ft of 8-inch diameter pipe?
questions.formula: gallons = 0.785 x diameter squared x length x 7.48 (convert inches to feet first)

questions.text: Water flows at 1.5 cfs through a 12-inch diameter pipe. What is the velocity?
questions.explanation: A 12-inch pipe is 1.0 ft in diameter. Area = 0.785 x 1 x 1 = 0.785 sq ft. Velocity = 1.5 / 0.785 = 1.9 ft/s.

questions.text: A flow of 2.0 MGD passes through a channel 2 ft wide with water 1.5 ft deep. What is the velocity in ft/s?
questions.explanation: 2.0 MGD x 1.547 = 3.094 cfs. Channel area = 2 x 1.5 = 3 sq ft. Velocity = 3.094 / 3 = 1.03, about 1.0 ft/s.
questions.formula: convert MGD to cfs (x 1.547), then velocity = flow / area

questions.text: Water moves at 2.0 ft/s through a channel with a cross-sectional area of 4 sq ft. What is the flow in cfs?
questions.explanation: Flow = velocity x area = 2.0 x 4 = 8 cfs.

questions.text: A 100,000-gallon tank receives a flow of 0.5 MGD. What is the detention time in hours?
A clearwell holds 50,000 gallons and the flow is 350 gpm. What is the detention time in hours?
A sedimentation basin holds 90,000 gallons and treats 0.6 MGD. What is the detention time in hours?
A plant feeds chlorine at 2.5 mg/L while treating 1.5 MGD. Using the pounds formula, what is the chlorine feed in pounds per day?
questions.explanation: lb/day = MGD x mg/L x 8.34 = 1.5 x 2.5 x 8.34 = 31.3 lb/day.

questions.text: A plant feeds 40 lb/day of chlorine to a flow of 2.0 MGD. What is the dosage in mg/L?
The chlorine dose is 3.0 mg/L and the measured residual is 0.6 mg/L. What is the chlorine demand?
questions.explanation: Demand = dose - residual = 3.0 - 0.6 = 2.4 mg/L.

questions.text: You need 30 lb/day of chlorine using calcium hypochlorite that is 65 percent available chlorine. How many lb/day of product is required?
questions.explanation: Product lb/day = chlorine needed / fraction available = 30 / 0.65 = 46.2 lb/day.

questions.text: A system feeds 50 lb/day of chlorine at a dosage of 2.5 mg/L. What is the flow in MGD?
How many lb/day of fluoride ion are needed to dose 2.0 MGD at 0.8 mg/L?
questions.explanation: lb/day = MGD x mg/L x 8.34 = 2.0 x 0.8 x 8.34 = 13.3 lb/day of fluoride ion.

questions.text: A clarifier surface is 1,500 sq ft and treats 0.5 MGD. What is the surface overflow rate in gpd/sq ft?
questions.explanation: Surface overflow rate = flow / area = 500,000 gpd / 1,500 sq ft = 333 gpd/sq ft.
questions.formula: surface overflow rate = flow (gpd) / surface area (sq ft)

questions.text: A clarifier has 200 ft of weir and discharges 0.6 MGD. What is the weir overflow rate in gpd/ft?
questions.explanation: Weir overflow rate = flow / weir length = 600,000 gpd / 200 ft = 3,000 gpd/ft.
questions.formula: weir overflow rate = flow (gpd) / weir length (ft)

questions.text: Flow to an aeration basin is 0.8 MGD with a BOD of 180 mg/L. What is the BOD loading in lb/day?
questions.explanation: lb/day = MGD x mg/L x 8.34 = 0.8 x 180 x 8.34 = 1,201, about 1,200 lb/day.

questions.text: A filter is 400 sq ft and treats 2.0 MGD. What is the filtration rate in gpm/sq ft?
questions.formula: filtration rate = flow (gpm) / filter area (sq ft); convert MGD to gpm first

questions.text: A treatment plant takes BOD from 200 mg/L in the influent down to 20 mg/L in the effluent. What percent removal is the plant achieving?
questions.explanation: Percent removal = (in - out) / in x 100 = (200 - 20) / 200 x 100 = 90 percent.

questions.text: Influent TSS is 240 mg/L and effluent TSS is 30 mg/L. What is the percent removal?
questions.explanation: Percent removal = (240 - 30) / 240 x 100 = 210 / 240 x 100 = 87.5 percent.

questions.text: A pump moves 500 gpm against 100 ft of total head. What is the water horsepower?
questions.explanation: Water horsepower = (gpm x head) / 3960 = (500 x 100) / 3960 = 12.6 whp.

questions.text: If the water horsepower is 12.6 and the pump efficiency is 80 percent, what is the brake horsepower?
questions.explanation: Brake horsepower = water hp / pump efficiency = 12.6 / 0.80 = 15.8 bhp.
questions.formula: brake hp = water hp / pump efficiency

questions.text: The brake horsepower is 15.8 and the motor efficiency is 90 percent. What is the motor input horsepower?
questions.explanation: Motor input hp = brake hp / motor efficiency = 15.8 / 0.90 = 17.6 hp.
questions.formula: motor input hp = brake hp / motor efficiency

questions.text: You blend 1.0 MGD of water at 300 mg/L hardness with 0.5 MGD of water at 0 mg/L hardness. What is the blended hardness?
questions.formula: blended concentration = (Q1 x C1 + Q2 x C2) / (Q1 + Q2)

questions.text: How many gallons of a 50 percent stock solution are needed to make 20 gallons of a 5 percent solution?
questions.explanation: C1 x V1 = C2 x V2. 50 x V1 = 5 x 20. V1 = 100 / 50 = 2 gallons of stock.
questions.formula: C1 x V1 = C2 x V2

questions.text: A well produces 200 gpm with a drawdown of 40 ft. What is the specific capacity in gpm/ft?
questions.explanation: Specific capacity = yield / drawdown = 200 / 40 = 5 gpm/ft.

questions.text: A booster station meters 2,500 gallons over a 5-minute run. What is the pumping rate in gpm?
questions.explanation: Rate is volume divided by time: 2,500 gallons / 5 minutes = 500 gpm.

questions.text: Flow through a meter vault reads 2.0 cfs. What is that in gallons per minute?
questions.explanation: One cfs is 448.8 gpm, so 2.0 cfs x 448.8 = about 898 gpm.

questions.text: A 1.0 MGD plant runs at a steady rate all day. About how many cubic feet per second is that?
questions.explanation: One MGD equals about 1.547 cfs, so a 1.0 MGD plant moves about 1.55 cfs.

questions.text: A day tank holds 300 gallons of water. Using the certification convention, how much does that water weigh?
questions.explanation: Water weighs 8.34 pounds per gallon by certification convention: 300 x 8.34 = 2,502 lb.

questions.text: A storage reservoir drops 2.0 acre-feet during a peak day. How many gallons left storage?
questions.explanation: One acre-foot is 325,851 gallons. 2.0 x 325,851 = about 652,000 gallons.

questions.text: A well pumps 250 gpm and runs 14 hours in a day. How much water did it produce that day?
questions.explanation: 250 gpm x 60 minutes x 14 hours = 210,000 gallons.

questions.text: A plant treats 480,000 gallons between midnight and 8:00 a.m. at a steady rate. If that pace holds all day, what is the daily flow in MGD?
questions.explanation: 480,000 gallons in 8 hours is 60,000 gallons per hour. Over 24 hours that is 1,440,000 gallons, which is 1.44 MGD.

questions.text: A storm drops 1.5 inches of rain on a 40-acre plant site. If every drop ran off, about how many gallons would that be?
questions.explanation: 1.5 inches is 0.125 feet. 0.125 ft x 40 acres = 5 acre-feet, and 5 x 325,851 = about 1.63 million gallons.

questions.text: A chlorinator feeds 42 pounds of gas over a full week of steady operation. What is the average feed in pounds per day?
questions.explanation: 42 pounds across 7 days is 42 / 7 = 6.0 pounds per day.

questions.text: A main flushes at 4.5 ft/s. How far does that water travel in one minute?
questions.explanation: Distance is velocity times time: 4.5 ft/s x 60 s = 270 feet every minute.

questions.text: A sludge drying bed measures 60 ft by 25 ft. What is its surface area?
questions.explanation: Rectangle area is length times width: 60 x 25 = 1,500 square feet.

questions.text: A clarifier is 50 ft in diameter. What is its surface area, to the nearest 10 square feet?
questions.explanation: Circle area is 0.785 x D squared: 0.785 x 50 x 50 = about 1,960 square feet.

questions.text: A chlorine contact basin is 30 ft long, 12 ft wide, and 10 ft deep. How many gallons does it hold when full?
questions.explanation: Volume is 30 x 12 x 10 = 3,600 cubic feet, and 3,600 x 7.48 = about 26,900 gallons.
questions.formula: gallons = L x W x D x 7.48

questions.text: A standpipe is 20 ft in diameter and currently holds 35 ft of water. About how many gallons is that?
questions.explanation: Cylinder volume is 0.785 x 20 x 20 x 35 = about 11,000 cubic feet. Times 7.48 gives about 82,200 gallons.
questions.formula: gallons = 0.785 x D^2 x depth x 7.48

questions.text: A rectangular basin 40 ft by 20 ft holds water 6 ft deep. The basin itself is 12 ft deep. How many gallons are in it right now?
questions.explanation: Use the water depth, not the wall height: 40 x 20 x 6 = 4,800 cubic feet, x 7.48 = about 35,900 gallons.
questions.formula: gallons = L x W x water depth x 7.48

questions.text: A chemical tote is a cylinder 4 ft in diameter and 5 ft tall. What is its volume in cubic feet?
questions.explanation: 0.785 x 4 x 4 x 5 = 62.8 cubic feet.

questions.text: Crews excavate a trench 120 ft long, 3 ft wide, and 5 ft deep. How many cubic yards of spoil come out?
questions.explanation: Volume is 120 x 3 x 5 = 1,800 cubic feet. A cubic yard is 27 cubic feet, so 1,800 / 27 = about 66.7 cubic yards.
questions.formula: cu yd = (L x W x D) / 27

questions.text: Water moves at 3.0 ft/s through a channel with a 2.5 square-foot cross-section. What is the flow?
questions.explanation: Flow is area times velocity: Q = 2.5 x 3.0 = 7.5 cfs.

questions.text: An 8-inch main carries 350 gpm. What is the velocity in the pipe?
questions.explanation: Convert flow: 350 / 448.8 = 0.78 cfs. Pipe area: 0.785 x (8/12)^2 = 0.35 sq ft. Velocity = 0.78 / 0.35 = about 2.2 ft/s.

questions.text: A 12-inch line flows full at 3.0 ft/s. About what flow is that in gpm?
questions.explanation: A 12-inch pipe has an area of 0.785 x 1 x 1 = 0.785 sq ft. Q = 0.785 x 3.0 = 2.36 cfs, and 2.36 x 448.8 = about 1,060 gpm.

questions.text: Flushing water moves through 1,200 feet of main at 2.5 ft/s. How long does it take the water to travel the full length?
questions.explanation: Time is distance over velocity: 1,200 / 2.5 = 480 seconds, which is 8 minutes.

questions.text: Flow stays constant as a main necks down from 12 inches to 6 inches. The 12-inch section runs at 2.0 ft/s. What is the velocity in the 6-inch section?
questions.explanation: Same flow through a quarter of the area means four times the velocity: 2.0 x 4 = 8.0 ft/s.

questions.text: A service line fills a 5-gallon bucket in 20 seconds. What is the flow in gpm?
questions.explanation: 20 seconds is a third of a minute, so the line delivers 5 x 3 = 15 gallons per minute.

questions.text: A rectangular channel 3 ft wide flows 18 inches deep at 2.0 ft/s. What is the flow in MGD?
questions.explanation: Area is 3 x 1.5 = 4.5 sq ft. Q = 4.5 x 2.0 = 9.0 cfs. One cfs is 0.6463 MGD, so 9.0 x 0.6463 = about 5.8 MGD.

questions.text: A clearwell holds 120,000 gallons and the plant pumps through it at 1,000 gpm. What is the detention time?
questions.explanation: Detention time is volume over flow: 120,000 / 1,000 = 120 minutes, which is 2 hours.

questions.text: An equalization basin holds 0.25 MG and receives a steady 1.2 MGD. What is the detention time in hours?
questions.explanation: 0.25 MG / 1.2 MGD = 0.208 days. Times 24 gives about 5 hours.

questions.text: A sedimentation basin is 80 ft long, 30 ft wide, and 12 ft deep, and treats 2.0 MGD. What is the detention time in hours?
questions.explanation: Volume is 80 x 30 x 12 x 7.48 = about 215,400 gallons. Flow is 2,000,000 / 24 = 83,333 gallons per hour. 215,400 / 83,333 = about 2.6 hours.
questions.formula: DT = (L x W x D x 7.48) / (gpd / 24)

questions.text: Operators want 30 minutes of contact time at a flow of 600 gpm. What tank volume does that take?
questions.explanation: Rearrange detention time: volume = flow x time = 600 gpm x 30 min = 18,000 gallons.

questions.text: A wet well holds 2,400 gallons between pump-off and pump-on. Inflow is 300 gpm with the pump off. How long until the pump starts?
questions.explanation: Fill time is volume over inflow: 2,400 / 300 = 8 minutes.

questions.text: A plant runs two clearwells in series, 40,000 gallons and 60,000 gallons, at a steady 500 gpm. What is the total detention time in hours?
questions.explanation: Tanks in series add their volumes: 100,000 gallons / 500 gpm = 200 minutes, which is about 3.3 hours.

questions.text: Flow through a 50,000-gallon contact tank rises from 200 gpm to 400 gpm. What is the new detention time?
questions.explanation: At 400 gpm: 50,000 / 400 = 125 minutes, which is about 2.1 hours. Twice the flow means half the contact time.

questions.text: A plant treats 1.0 MGD at a chlorine dose of 3.0 mg/L. How many pounds of chlorine per day is that?
questions.explanation: The pounds formula: lb/day = MGD x mg/L x 8.34 = 1.0 x 3.0 x 8.34 = about 25 lb/day.

questions.text: An operator feeds 42 lb/day of chlorine into a flow of 1.8 MGD. What dose is the water receiving?
questions.explanation: Rearrange the pounds formula: mg/L = lb/day / (MGD x 8.34) = 42 / (1.8 x 8.34) = about 2.8 mg/L.

questions.text: A 0.5 MGD system needs a 6.0 mg/L dose. The hypochlorite on hand is 12.5 percent available chlorine. How many pounds of hypochlorite solution per day?
questions.explanation: Pure chlorine: 0.5 x 6.0 x 8.34 = 25 lb/day. The product is only 12.5 percent chlorine, so 25 / 0.125 = 200 lb of solution per day.
questions.formula: lb product = (MGD x mg/L x 8.34) / strength

questions.text: In the pounds formula, what does the 8.34 represent?
choices: The gallons in one cubic foot
The weight of one gallon of water in pounds
questions.explanation: One gallon of water weighs 8.34 pounds. That is why flow in MGD times concentration in mg/L times 8.34 gives pounds per day.

questions.text: Chlorine demand is 2.2 mg/L and the operator wants a 0.8 mg/L free residual leaving the plant. At 1.5 MGD, what feed rate does that take?
questions.explanation: Dose = demand + residual = 2.2 + 0.8 = 3.0 mg/L. Then 1.5 x 3.0 x 8.34 = about 38 lb/day.
questions.formula: dose = demand + residual; lb/day = MGD x mg/L x 8.34

questions.text: A fluoride system doses 0.9 mg/L into 750,000 gallons per day. How many pounds of fluoride ion is that per day?
questions.explanation: 750,000 gpd is 0.75 MGD. 0.75 x 0.9 x 8.34 = about 5.6 lb/day.

questions.text: A polymer feed runs at 1.2 mg/L on a 2.0 MGD flow. What is the feed rate in pounds per day?
questions.explanation: 1.2 x 2.0 x 8.34 = about 20 lb/day.

questions.text: A chlorinator ran at 30 lb/day for 12 hours, then sat idle the rest of the day. Total flow for the full day was 1.0 MG. What average dose did the day's water receive?
questions.explanation: At 30 lb/day for 12 hours the feeder delivered 15 pounds. Dose = 15 / (1.0 x 8.34) = about 1.8 mg/L.

questions.text: Liquid hypochlorite at 12.5 percent carries about 1.04 pounds of available chlorine per gallon. How many gallons does it take to feed 13 pounds of chlorine?
questions.explanation: Gallons = pounds needed / pounds per gallon = 13 / 1.04 = about 12.5 gallons.
questions.formula: gallons = lb needed / lb per gallon

questions.text: A new well needs disinfecting at 50 mg/L. The well casing and column hold 4,000 gallons of water. How many pounds of chlorine is that?
questions.explanation: 4,000 gallons is 0.004 MG. 0.004 x 50 x 8.34 = about 1.7 pounds of chlorine.

questions.text: Dosing 1.0 mg/L into 1.0 million gallons of water takes how many pounds of chemical?
questions.explanation: That is the pounds formula at its simplest: 1.0 MG x 1.0 mg/L x 8.34 = 8.34 pounds.

questions.text: A plant fed 62 pounds of alum while treating 2.5 million gallons. What was the alum dose?
A 60-ft diameter clarifier treats 1.5 MGD. What is the surface overflow rate?
questions.explanation: Surface area is 0.785 x 60 x 60 = 2,826 sq ft. SOR = 1,500,000 / 2,826 = about 530 gpd per square foot.

questions.text: A circular clarifier has 188 feet of weir and passes 1.2 MGD. What is the weir overflow rate?
questions.explanation: Weir overflow rate is flow over weir length: 1,200,000 / 188 = about 6,400 gpd per foot of weir.

questions.text: A filter box measures 20 ft by 20 ft and passes 1,200 gpm. What is the filtration rate?
questions.explanation: Filter area is 20 x 20 = 400 sq ft. Rate = 1,200 / 400 = 3.0 gpm per square foot.

questions.text: Backwash on a 400-square-foot filter runs at 6,000 gpm. What is the backwash rate?
questions.explanation: Backwash rate is backwash flow over filter area: 6,000 / 400 = 15 gpm per square foot.

questions.text: A lagoon receives 0.3 MGD with an influent BOD of 200 mg/L. What is the organic loading in pounds of BOD per day?
questions.explanation: Organic loading uses the pounds formula: 0.3 x 200 x 8.34 = about 500 pounds of BOD per day.

questions.text: A trickling filter 50 ft in diameter receives 0.5 MGD including recirculation. What is the hydraulic loading?
questions.explanation: Area is 0.785 x 50 x 50 = about 1,960 sq ft. Loading = 500,000 / 1,960 = about 255 gpd per square foot.

questions.text: A 250,000-gallon basin receives 1.0 MGD. How many times does the basin volume turn over in a day?
questions.explanation: Turnovers per day = daily flow / basin volume = 1,000,000 / 250,000 = 4.

questions.text: A gravity thickener 30 ft in diameter receives 60,000 gpd of sludge at 8,000 mg/L solids. What is the solids loading in pounds per day per square foot?
questions.explanation: Solids: 0.06 MGD x 8,000 x 8.34 = about 4,000 lb/day. Area: 0.785 x 30 x 30 = 706 sq ft. Loading = 4,000 / 706 = about 5.7.

questions.text: A grit channel removes 45 of the 50 pounds of grit that enter it each day. What is the removal efficiency?
questions.explanation: Removal is what came out over what went in: 45 / 50 = 0.90, which is 90 percent.

questions.text: Influent TSS is 210 mg/L and the primary clarifier effluent runs 126 mg/L. What percent is the primary removing?
questions.explanation: Removal = (in - out) / in = (210 - 126) / 210 = 0.40, which is 40 percent.

questions.text: A filter takes turbidity from 4.0 NTU down to 0.2 NTU. What is the percent removal?
questions.explanation: Removal = (4.0 - 0.2) / 4.0 = 0.95, which is 95 percent.

questions.text: Two processes in series each remove 50 percent of incoming solids. What is the overall removal across both?
questions.explanation: The first stage leaves 50 percent. The second removes half of THAT, leaving 25 percent. Overall removal is 75 percent.

questions.text: A permit requires 85 percent BOD removal. Influent BOD is 240 mg/L. What is the highest effluent BOD that still complies?
questions.explanation: If 85 percent must come out, 15 percent may remain: 240 x 0.15 = 36 mg/L.
questions.formula: max effluent = influent x (1 - required removal)

questions.text: A pressure gauge at the base of a standpipe reads 26 psi. How high is the water above the gauge?
questions.explanation: Each psi supports 2.31 feet of water: 26 x 2.31 = about 60 feet.

questions.text: A tank's water surface sits 92.4 feet above a home's meter. Ignoring friction, what static pressure does the home see?
questions.explanation: Pressure is head divided by 2.31: 92.4 / 2.31 = 40 psi.

questions.text: An elevated tank's overflow sits at 165 ft, with the water level 15 ft below overflow. What is the static pressure at a hydrant at elevation zero?
questions.explanation: Actual water level is 165 - 15 = 150 ft. Pressure = 150 / 2.31 = about 65 psi.

questions.text: One psi of pressure pushes a column of water up about how far?
questions.explanation: One psi equals 2.31 feet of water column. Its inverse, 0.433 psi per foot, is the same fact turned around.

questions.text: A pump's suction gauge reads 5 psi and its discharge gauge reads 65 psi. How much head is the pump adding?
questions.explanation: The pump adds 65 - 5 = 60 psi, and 60 x 2.31 = about 139 feet of head.
questions.formula: head added = (discharge psi - suction psi) x 2.31

questions.text: A zone's tank water level sits at 180 ft elevation. A pressure-reducing valve sits at 30 ft elevation. What static pressure arrives at the valve?
questions.explanation: Head at the valve is 180 - 30 = 150 ft. Pressure = 150 / 2.31 = about 65 psi.

questions.text: Using the 0.433 factor, what is the pressure at the bottom of a standpipe holding 80 feet of water?
questions.explanation: Pressure = 80 x 0.433 = about 35 psi. Same answer as dividing by 2.31; the two factors are inverses.

questions.text: A gauge reads 48 psi at an elevation of 60 ft. What is the hydraulic grade line elevation at that point?
questions.explanation: Convert pressure to head: 48 x 2.31 = about 111 ft. Add the gauge elevation: 111 + 60 = about 171 ft. That is the HGL.

questions.text: A lift station pump raises 400 gpm through 80 feet of total head. What water horsepower is the pump putting into the water?
questions.explanation: Water horsepower = gpm x head / 3,960 = 400 x 80 / 3,960 = about 8.1.

questions.text: What does the 3,960 in the water horsepower formula do?
choices: Converts gpm times feet of head into horsepower
Accounts for the weight of chlorine
questions.explanation: It is the unit conversion: 33,000 ft-lb per minute per horsepower divided by 8.34 lb per gallon gives 3,960. Flow times head divided by it yields horsepower.

questions.text: A pump must produce 10 water horsepower and the pump itself is 70 percent efficient. What brake horsepower must the motor deliver to the shaft?
questions.explanation: Brake horsepower = water horsepower / pump efficiency = 10 / 0.70 = about 14.3.

questions.text: Brake horsepower is 12 and the motor is 90 percent efficient. What horsepower does the motor draw from the power line?
questions.explanation: Input power = brake horsepower / motor efficiency = 12 / 0.90 = about 13.3 horsepower.

questions.text: A motor nameplate reads 25 hp. About how many kilowatts is that at full output?
questions.explanation: One horsepower is 0.746 kilowatts: 25 x 0.746 = about 18.7 kW.

questions.text: A pump moves 600 gpm at 100 feet of head with a wire-to-water efficiency of 65 percent. What horsepower is being drawn from the line?
questions.explanation: Water horsepower = 600 x 100 / 3,960 = about 15.2. Wire-to-water covers all losses, so line draw = 15.2 / 0.65 = about 23.3 horsepower.
questions.formula: line hp = (gpm x ft / 3960) / wire-to-water eff

questions.text: Two identical pumps run in parallel against the same head. Each delivers 300 gpm at that head. About what total flow do they deliver together?
questions.explanation: In parallel, flows add at the same head: 300 + 300 = about 600 gpm (slightly less in a real system as head rises with flow).
questions.formula: parallel: total gpm = sum of pump flows

questions.text: You need 50 gallons of a 1 percent solution, mixed down from 12.5 percent hypochlorite. How many gallons of the 12.5 percent product do you start with?
questions.explanation: C1 x V1 = C2 x V2. V1 = (1 x 50) / 12.5 = 4 gallons of strong product, then fill to 50 with water.

questions.text: One pump fills a reservoir in 6 hours. A second identical pump joins it. About how long does filling take now?
questions.explanation: Two identical pumps double the fill rate, so the time halves: 6 / 2 = 3 hours.

questions.text: A system blends 0.5 MGD of well water carrying 0.8 mg/L of a mineral with 1.5 MGD of surface water carrying 0.2 mg/L of the same mineral. What is the blended concentration?
questions.explanation: Weight each source by its flow: (0.5 x 0.8 + 1.5 x 0.2) / (0.5 + 1.5) = 0.7 / 2.0 = 0.35 mg/L.
questions.formula: blend = sum(flow x conc) / total flow

questions.text: Sludge runs 4 percent solids. How many pounds of dry solids are in 1,000 gallons of that sludge?
questions.explanation: 1,000 gallons weighs about 8,340 pounds. Four percent of that is dry solids: 8,340 x 0.04 = about 334 pounds.
questions.formula: dry lb = gallons x 8.34 x % solids

questions.text: The plant uses 3 gallons of polymer for every 100,000 gallons treated. How much polymer does 1.5 million gallons take?
questions.explanation: 1.5 million is fifteen 100,000-gallon blocks: 3 x 15 = 45 gallons.

questions.text: A make-down calls for 1 gallon of chemical per 100 gallons of water. How many gallons of chemical go into a 5,000-gallon batch of water?
questions.explanation: 5,000 / 100 = 50 batches of the ratio, so 50 gallons of chemical.
